# **Improving Spark Shuffle Reliability**

Status: *Done*  Author: [Yi Chen](mailto:yichen@stripe.com)    Created: May 20, 2024  Last Updated: Aug 1, 2024

| 2024-06-11 | Shared this doc in \#team-data-infra, see [thread](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CJFHE7AR3/p1718146768066259) |
| :---- | :---- |
| 2024-06-17 | The DI Team met and reviewed this doc. See [next steps](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CJFHE7AR3/p1718654823027229?thread_ts=1718643637.660199&cid=CJFHE7AR3).Multi-EBS volumes enabled for staging-analytics-emr6 ([PR](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/pull/6406)) |
| 2024-06-19 | Short-term solution (spark.network.timeout,  spark.shuffle.io.connectionTimeout) rolled out to all clusters |
| 2024-06-21 | Multi-EBS volumes enabled for prod-dma-emr6 ([PR](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/pull/6443)) |
| 2024-06-24 | Multi-EBS volumes enabled for prod-data-platform-emr6 ([PR](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/pull/6459)) and backfill-emr6 ([PR](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/pull/6457)) |
| 2024-08-02 | Multi-EBS volumes enabled for prod-etl-emr6 ([PR](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/pull/6466)) |

# Summary

The current Spark shuffling mechanism is known to have reliability and performance challenges, and the remote shuffling as the solution has been on the roadmap since 2018 for the open-source Spark project ([SPARK-25299](https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/SPARK-25299)). This has become a reliability risk recently in our Spark infrastructure. In May, we saw [80% of Spark/EMR reliability issues are caused by the shuffle reliability](#heading=h.7jy02ilta3rv). Improving shuffle reliability is the single biggest investment we can do now to improve the Spark infrastructure reliability. If we are able to adopt a remote shuffle service (Apache Celeborn or equivalent) successfully, not only we expect to reduce the Spark related escals by 80% (with improved reliability), we also expect better job performance, and [a potential $1.3 million annual savings](#how-much-can-we-potentially-save-for-using-a-remote-shuffle-service?).

In this doc, we will cover some background knowledge about how Spark shuffle works and what are the risks of the current infrastructure, [and how to diagnose this issue](#how-to-diagnose-a-shuffle-hotspot-issue?).  I propose [a few short-term solutions](#short-term-proposal) that can land within the 2025 Q2 timeframe , and also propose [a long-term solution](#long-term-solution) that is more involved, but can potentially bring about significant reliability, performance and cost improvements. This comes with risks and also requires proper prioritization.

# Problem

## 80% of Spark reliability issues are shuffle related issues

The following table shows all escals for the Data Infra team that are related to Spark/EMR reliabilities. Security configurations, permission related issues are not included. Quarry issues are also not included because it runs on k8s.

From this table, the rows marked with green blocks are shuffled reliability issues.  It is clear that about 80% of Spark reliability issues in May are shuffle reliability related issues. 

| Is Shuffle  | date | Issue and Slack Link | Comments |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
|  | May 21, 2024 | [A specific task (search\_ctr\_messags\_all) across a few of our signal\_service\_ DAGs has been failing since 05-09](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1716311473633149) | This issue took 20 days to resolve. Increasing the timeouts, moving the job to an all-ondemand cluster did not help. Eventually we identified this is a [self-inflicted issue by the Spark job](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1717637559581649?thread_ts=1716311473.633149&cid=C01FB307Z7A). The [causes](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1718030651741899?thread_ts=1716311473.633149&cid=C01FB307Z7A) include (1) the application was doing [coalesce(1)](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1718031180079769?thread_ts=1716311473.633149&cid=C01FB307Z7A) which transfers all data into one machine and creates a hotspot, (2) the application persists datasets to local disks (which may have side effects) |
|  | May 29, 2024 | [Looks like jobs-spark3.jar is cached and not using the latest build](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1717020627795999) | HDFS caching |
|  | May 28, 2024  | [I am working on optimising a prod job which is failing for quite a few days due to spark FetchfailedException earlier now Future timed out issue.](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1716923019998189) | Recommended to increase spark.network.timeout to 240s |
|  | May 19, 2024 | [We have been struggling with persistent memory issues in our Spark jobs over the past week, causing frequent failures and a significant backlog of pending jobs](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1716131133375199) | Broadcast shuffle. It may be caused by either spot interruption, or by shuffle hotspot issues. DEA cluster jobs are not in good state (need significant tuning) |
|  | May 15, 2024 | [A few MF tasks failed while seeing same message Configuration: resource-types.xml not found.](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715789194185529) | Should be resolved by the HDFS caching. |
|  | May 17, 2024 | [We are having failures in revenue\_metrics\_v1 and sales\_audit\_automation dag tasks this week due to memory issue](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715984161951029) | Enabled shuffle compression and io compression |
|  | May 16, 2024 | [Livy jobs failing due to lack of YARN resources](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715873273879039) | Large shuffle size caused DataNode to be marked as unhealthy in DMA cluster. Enabled shuffle compression to reduce the shuffle file size footprint.  |
|  | May 16, 2024 | [prod-etl-emr6 appears to be at capacity and not scaling out further, causing delays across multiple pipelines](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715869202379219) | Large shuffle size caused DataNode to be marked as unhealthy in etl cluster. Enabled shuffle compression to reduce shuffle file size Asked the job owner to tune a job with large shuffle |
|  | May 13, 2024 | [We have a job running into repeated lost executor from bad node failures on prod-etl-emr6](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715628074044779) | Identified [large shuffle skew of 100x ratio](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715643433765729?thread_ts=1715628074.044779&cid=C01FB307Z7A), potentially the root cause for shuffle hotspot.  User changed the job by [removing the order-by](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715711509406909?thread_ts=1715628074.044779&cid=C01FB307Z7A) operation which removed the aggregation shuffle. Problem solved. |
|  | May 11, 2024 | [merlin\_agg\_apps is running a backfill and the first tasks that started yesterday where running in a good pace. But after I checked this morning it is going extremely slow](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715442922922879) | Large backfill, experiencing [shuffle write time longer than task execution time](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715720854108649?thread_ts=1715442922.922879&cid=C01FB307Z7A), caused by shuffle hotspot.  Removed spot instances, running backfill on all on-demand instances on dedicated backfill cluster. Still [experiencing shuffle hotspot issue](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715807369202619?thread_ts=1715442922.922879&cid=C01FB307Z7A). [Enabled shuffle compression](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715812021532249?thread_ts=1715442922.922879&cid=C01FB307Z7A) which resolved the shuffle issue, backfill was successful. As a result, the shuffle compression is now enabled for all clusters by default. ,  |
|  | May 6, 2024  | [prod-etl-emr6 appears to be having issues this morning will similar submit errors to the incident that popped up over the weekend.](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715002224892559) | Related to [shuffle fetch failure](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715012488873799?thread_ts=1715002224.892559&cid=C01FB307Z7A) |
|  | May 3, 2024 | [We are seeing quarry errors and our DAGs are stuck.](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1714757110646469) | Root cause was the jobs are running on spot instances. Spot interruption triggered shuffle file loss. |
|  | May 1, 2024 | [I've had the same spark job fail twice with the message: MetadataFetchFailedException: Unable to deserialize broadcasted map statuses for shuffle](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1714593044619229) | Looks like a shuffle issue, but this issue was not closed for some reason. |
|  | May 1, 2024 | [Airflow jobs are unable to start due to memory limit.](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1714575332432629) | Changes not applied to Gov. Fixed by apply new core limit PR to Gov |

## Root Causes and Impacts

We use the External Shuffle Service in our Spark infrastructure. This is an auxiliary service running on each NodeManager. It is a requirement for enabling dynamic allocation for Spark jobs. When an executor is deallocated by the dynamic allocation, the shuffle files will be served by the External Shuffle Service running on the host. This service runs on port 7337 on each machine.

In general, the Spark shuffle consumes about 15% of cluster resources. When a mapper writes the shuffle file, it needs to order the data locally before writing, which consumes memory. If memory is not enough, the mapper will spill the data to disk, which has a 3x write amplification, which wastes more resources from slow IO. When a reducer reads shuffle files, it is also inefficient to read thousands of concurrent requests to read small amounts of data with random read. Such low efficiency IO further wastes expensive cluster resources like memory and cpus.

When the shuffle file becomes too large, either because the job involves large shuffle, or there is a significant data skew, it also creates significant reliability problems for the machines involved. For example, the NodeManager may run out of disk space, and as a result, the YARN will mark it as unhealthy and remove it from the cluster, which impacts all applications that are running on that machine. If these machines are still considered healthy, a large number of network connections, shuffle data reads and writes, can overwhelm the machine, which will slow down all tasks involved.

The following is an example of a job that is impacted by shuffle reliability in the prod-data-platform-emr6 cluster. Notice that each task spends more time in shuffle read (orange bar) than execution (green bar). One of the task running on executor \#72, spent 1.9 minutes in shuffle writing (while execution time was only 20 seconds). **All these slow and inefficient IO from shuffle reads and writes generated waste of more expensive cluster resources like memory and cpu because these resources are mostly idle during the IO.**

 

![][image1]

The above example shows that all tasks are affected by large shuffles on all executors, this following [example](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715720854108649?thread_ts=1715442922.922879&cid=C01FB307Z7A) shows how a few number of hotspots impact a job (see [escal-data](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1715442922922879)). Notice the long shuffle writes (yellow bar) for some tasks. The reason this is a shuffle hotspot issue is because Spark driver will assign tasks to executors randomly. Although we expect some data skew that can result in longer shuffle write time, we would only see such long shuffle write time randomly distributed across executors. Instead of random distribution, we see a clear pattern that all tasks running on a few executors (1st, 2nd, 3rd, 5th in this chart) have extra long shuffle write time, while all tasks on all other executors have ignorable shuffle write time. These 4 impacted executors are running on 2 distinct hosts. This is a clear sign that these two machines are overwhelmed by shuffling, and likely all Spark applications running on them are affected. Also note that this is not necessarily an infrastructure issue. It is possible that the job itself moved most data into a few machines which caused this issue.

![][image2]

# Proposals

## Short-Term Proposal {#short-term-proposal}

The short-term solutions that has been deployed:

* Enable shuffle compression for all clusters. This will reduce the file size of shuffle files, and reduce the risk of NodeManager becoming unhealthy because large shuffle files use up local disk (EBS) space. It can reduce the amount of data transferred through the network during the shuffle process.  
* Only use large EC2 instances in critical clusters. We are only using 12xlarge and 16xlarge machines for prod-dma-emr6, prod-data-platform-emr6 and prod-etl-emr6 clusters. We provision EBS volume size linearly to the machine’s core count, so larger machines have larger EBS volume and network bandwidth. We assume that larger volume makes the machine tolerate shuffle file size skew batter. 

The short-term solutions that are currently planned in Q2 2024:

* Increase spark.network.timeout value from default 120s to 240s (and potentially 600s).  
* Increase spark.shuffle.io.connectionTimeout to 480s  
* Use 4 EBS volumes per 16xlarge nodes instead of 1, but maintain the total EBS volume size the same, which increases aggregated EBS throughput by 4x per node (see [AWS example](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/ebs-optimized.html), [example PR](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/pull/6379), [search-cluster](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/pull/6380)).  
* If these changes are not helping, we will recommend the users to tune their jobs (see [example](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1718031180079769?thread_ts=1716311473.633149&cid=C01FB307Z7A)):  
  * Is your job calling the .**persist**() method to persist large datasets to local disk?  
  * Is your job using **coalesse()** large number of partitions into a very small number of machines?  
  * Are you doing expensive operations that require sortings locally? Think **orderBy()**.  
  * Is your job too large with repeated processing of the same dataset? In that case, split the job into smaller pieces.

## Long-Term Solution {#long-term-solution}

For the long-term, I recommend we **evaluate a remote shuffle service solution, and decide if we should adopt one**. There are a few different options now, and Apache Celeborn is the most popular among the open-source solutions. It graduated from the incubator status into a full Apache project in April 2024\.

The preferred approach is to adopt Apache Celeborn for the following reasons:

* Apache Celeborn improves **reliability**  
  * The issues we experienced when nodes become unhealthy due to large shuffle file footprint will be eliminated. This is because with Apache Celeborn, the shuffle data will be pushed directly from the Spark executors to the remote Celeborn worker, and we can choose to not write to the attached EBS volume (which saves both disk space and network bandwidth).  
  * The shuffle hotspot issue will be reduced. Apache Celeborn does reorganization of the data. For shuffle files that are too large it can generate splits and balance them across nodes.  
  * Apache Celeborn can host a replica for better reliability and performance.  
* Apache Celeborn improves **performance**  
  * There are a lot fewer shuffle files stored on Apache Celeborn. There is only one shuffle file to read from each Spark reducer task, instead of m files (m is the number of mappers). Sequential reads are much faster than random reads.  
  * Apache Celeborn reduces local-disk shuffle spill. Shuffle spill has a 3x write amplification factor and can slow down the application by making it IO bound. Not only that, Apache Celeborn can also improve executor’s disk spill from computation (besides shuffle spill)  
* Apache Celeborn will be much **cheaper**. We expect 80% savings on EBS cost, plus less demand on cluster resources (TODO: link to the reference of savings)  
  * It allows us to disaggregate the compute from storage, and allows the compute layer and storage layer to scale independently. We can continue to use r6g and r7g instances without local storage, and the shuffle writes are directly pushed to Celeborn.   
  * The size of the shuffle files is also reduced by 40% because Celeborn will translate the row-based shuffle file to column-based shuffle file.  
  * Significant reduction of slow IO, means much less waste of memory and cpu resources during the slow IO. The jobs will finish faster with less aggregated memory and cpu resource consumption. 

The main risk of adopting Apache Celeborn at Slack:

* [Rahul Gidwani](mailto:rgidwani@salesforce.com): [small user base](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1717613094220599?thread_ts=1716311473.633149&cid=C01FB307Z7A).  
* A new surface area that the team needs to learn and support.

# F.A.Q

## What is the community doing with this problem?

[SPARK-25299](https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/SPARK-25299): using remote storage for shuffle data, has been opened since 2018\.

## How to diagnose a shuffle hotspot issue? {#how-to-diagnose-a-shuffle-hotspot-issue?}

In most cases, you will see FetchFailedException, or MetadataFetchFailedException, or Failed to get blocks.

### *ERROR ShufleBlockFeetcherIterator: Failed to get blocks, caused by Timeout waiting for task*

This is the most common error our users saw. Traditionally it was believed that it’s caused either by spot instances taken away, or EMR being too aggressive when decommissioning nodes. The key to identifying the shuffle hotspot issue is the cause: “Timeout waiting for task.” If a FetchFailedException is caused by “Timeout waiting for task”, it’s most likely a shuffle reliability issue, not caused by spot instances, or EMR decommissioning.

![][image3]

### *ERROR ShufleBlockFeetcherIterator: Failed to get blocks, caused by Connection from external shuffle service closed*

The following screenshot shows a typical ERROR message from ShufleBlockFeetcherIterator. It shows that a shuffle fetch failed, because the network connection to the external shuffle service was closed. The reason we know this is a connection to the external shuffle service is by looking at the port number 7337\. 

![][image4]

### *MetadataFetchFailedException: Unable to deserialize broadcasted map status for shuffle*

The current hypothesis is that we get this error because broadcasted data is transferred via a torrent-like mechanism, and when the shuffling saturates the network and cause the network interface to drop packets, causing the failure to deserialize the data.

![][image5]

## 

## ![][image6]

## How much can we potentially save for using a remote shuffle service? {#how-much-can-we-potentially-save-for-using-a-remote-shuffle-service?}

In May 2024, we spent roughly $130k on EBS. Online techtalk suggested that Apache Celeborn can save 85% of cost for local disks (TODO: link to the reference here). This translates to $1.3 million potential annual savings.

![][image7]

According to [this chart for a selected list of improved EMR clusters](https://us-east-1.console.aws.amazon.com/cost-management/home?region=us-east-1#/cost-explorer?chartStyle=STACK&costAggregate=netAmortizedCost&endDate=2024-05-31&excludeForecasting=false&filter=%5B%7B%22dimension%22:%7B%22id%22:%22CostCategoryName%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Cost%20category%22%7D,%22operator%22:%22INCLUDES%22,%22values%22:%5B%7B%22value%22:%22Data%20Infrastructure%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Data%20Infrastructure%22%7D%5D,%22growableValue%22:%7B%22value%22:%22Automatic%20-%20Omni%20Team%20Name%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Automatic%20-%20Omni%20Team%20Name%22%7D%7D,%7B%22dimension%22:%7B%22id%22:%22TagKey%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Tag%22%7D,%22operator%22:%22INCLUDES%22,%22values%22:%5B%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform2-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform2-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform4-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform4-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform3-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform3-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-dma-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-dma-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-dma2-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-dma2-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-dma-temp-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-dma-temp-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-etl-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-etl-emr6%22%7D%5D,%22growableValue%22:%7B%22value%22:%22Name%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Name%22%7D%7D%5D&futureRelativeRange=CUSTOM&granularity=Monthly&groupBy=%5B%22Service%22%5D&historicalRelativeRange=CUSTOM&isDefault=false&reportArn=arn:aws:ce::429538831549:ce-saved-report%2F1a327863-5a1b-4929-844b-eaafa0407948&reportId=1a327863-5a1b-4929-844b-eaafa0407948&reportName=New%20cost%20and%20usage%20report&showOnlyUncategorized=false&showOnlyUntagged=false&startDate=2024-02-01&usageAggregate=undefined&useNormalizedUnits=false), we are spending $59573 in EBS while $240937 for EC2. That is, the EBS cost is about a quarter of EC2 cost. We expect to save 50%-80% of EBS cost with a remote shuffle service. This means that we can potentially save 7% \- 12% of total EMR cost (including EC2, EBS, and EMR fees).

![][image8]

## Why now? Did the cost saving effort create this reliability risk?

Spark Shuffle with local storage and its reliability is a well-known challenge when Spark infrastructure grows bigger and more complicated. [SPARK-25299](https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/SPARK-25299) has been opened since 2018 to address this problem. It is also more complicated because it is difficult to identify the offender and the victim in a multi-tenant environment. For example, in this [escal](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1716311473633149), there was no obvious shuffle skew and other symptoms based on Spark UI, and it is only when a test job was killed manually and through grafana chart analysis can we identify this job itself is the cause of the shuffle issues. In the past, we had some well-known infrastructure reliability issues like spot interruptions, and some misconception of EMR autoscaling being too aggressive in decommission nodes, so it is very easy to mistake FetchFailedException as the result of these causes.

**As part of cost saving effort, we also optimized some clusters by removing undesirable nodes like c6g, c7g**. These nodes waste about 50%-80% of the CPU cores. The number of CPU cores is the number of parallel tasks it can run in the best cases, so these undesirable instances may have created a throttling effect, by wasting 80% of cores, it limits the number of tasks it can run, hence hiding the impact of shuffle issues. Removing these instances and improving the resource utilization may have allowed the shuffle reliability issue to surface. However, this is only for the average case. **In this [escal](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1716311473633149), we brought back the c6g and c7g instances to test if it helps resolving the issue, and it didn’t**. We can conclude that in general sense, more resource utilization can surface this issue, and we can address that by the proposed short-term solutions, but that won’t solve the issue if the job itself is not healthy.
